abstract: Community nursing serves as a fundamental pillar of the Ecuadorian public health system, playing a decisive role in health promotion and disease prevention across both rural and marginalized urban landscapes. This study examines the multifaceted contributions of community nurses, emphasizing their leadership in health education, nationwide immunization initiatives, and the comprehensive management of chronic conditions. Evidence suggests that targeted educational interventions have significantly improved lifestyle behaviors, leading to a marked decrease in the incidence of childhood malnutrition and gastrointestinal infections. Furthermore, community outreach has expanded vaccination coverage in underserved regions and enhanced therapeutic adherence among patients with non-communicable diseases, thereby improving overall quality of life. Despite these successes, the profession grapples with systemic barriers, including precarious infrastructure, chronic resource shortages, and limited access to specialized continuing education. This paper argues that strengthening community nursing in Ecuador requires the integration of innovative digital tools, the development of robust public policies, and the fostering of intersectoral partnerships. Addressing these challenges is essential to maximizing the impact of nursing interventions and achieving more equitable public health outcomes nationwide.
